title: The Kippah (Yarmulke): Why Jewish Men Cover Their Heads
title_zh: 基帕(圆顶小帽):犹太男子为何要遮盖头部
title_he: הכיפה (יארמולקא): מדוע גברים יהודים מכסים את ראשם
author: Torah Light Team
target_audience: Chinese Mandarin speakers with no prior Jewish knowledge
key_concepts: Not a Torah commandment but rabbinic tradition
Cultural parallels to Chinese imperial hats
cultural_parallels: Chinese imperial court hats denoting rank and respect
Buddhist monks' head practices as humility symbols
Concept of 'heaven above' (天) in Chinese philosophy
practical_applications: When Orthodox men wear kippot (all times)
How to keep a kippah on (clips, bobby pins, friction)
Wearing kippah in workplace and public
Teaching children to wear kippah from age 3
No blessing when putting on kippah
historical_context: Talmudic period origins (circa 500 CE, Northern-Southern Dynasties China)
sources: Rabbi Aryeh Kaplan - The Aryeh Kaplan Reader
